    β = zeros(p)
    σ² = 1.0
    
    # Precompute quantities
    Σ₀_inv = inv(Σ₀)
    XtX = X'X
    Xty = X'y
    Σ₀_inv_μ₀ = Σ₀_inv * μ₀
    
    for i in 1:n_samples
        # Update β | σ², y
        Σₙ_inv = Σ₀_inv + XtX / σ²
        Σₙ = inv(Σₙ_inv)
        μₙ = Σₙ * (Σ₀_inv_μ₀ + Xty / σ²)
        β = rand(MvNormal(μₙ, Σₙ))
        
        # Update σ² | β, y
        residuals = y - X * β
        bₙ = b₀ + dot(residuals, residuals) / 2
        aₙ = a₀ + n / 2
        σ² = rand(InverseGamma(aₙ, bₙ))
        
        # Store samples
        β_samples[:, i] = β
        σ²_samples[i] = σ²
    end
    
    return β_samples, σ²_samples
end

# Simulate data
Random.seed!(42)
n, p = 100, 3
X = [ones(n) randn(n, p-1)]  # Include intercept
β_true = [2.0, -1.5, 0.8]
σ²_true = 0.5
y = X * β_true + sqrt(σ²_true) * randn(n)

# Prior specification
μ₀ = zeros(p)
Σ₀ = 10.0 * I(p)  # Weakly informative prior
a₀, b₀ = 1.0, 1.0  # Weakly informative inverse gamma

# Run Gibbs sampler
β_samples, σ²_samples = gibbs_linear_regression(y, X, 5000, μ₀, Σ₀, a₀, b₀)

# Posterior summaries (after burn-in)
burn_in = 1000
β_posterior_means = mean(β_samples[:, burn_in+1:end], dims=2)
σ²_posterior_mean = mean(σ²_samples[burn_in+1:end])

println("Posterior means:")
println("β: ", round.(vec(β_posterior_means), digits=3))
println("σ²: ", round(σ²_posterior_mean, digits=3))
println("\nTrue values:")
println("β: ", β_true)
println("σ²: ", σ²_true)

4.6 Convergence Theory

The theoretical foundation of MCMC rests on ergodic theory, which provides conditions under which sample averages converge to posterior expectations.

Definition 4.6 (Ergodicity). A Markov chain is ergodic if it is irreducible, aperiodic, and has a unique stationary distribution π.

For ergodic chains, the ergodic theorem guarantees almost sure convergence:

Theorem 4.4 (Ergodic Theorem). If {Xₙ} is an ergodic Markov chain with stationary distribution π, then for any integrable function g,

1/n ∑ᵢ₌₁ⁿ g(Xᵢ) → ∫ g(x)π(dx) almost surely

4.6.1 Geometric Ergodicity and Central Limit Theorems

Stronger convergence results require geometric ergodicity, which ensures exponential decay to stationarity.

Definition 4.7 (Geometric Ergodicity). A Markov chain is geometrically ergodic if there exist constants C < ∞ and ρ < 1 such that

‖Pⁿ(x, ·) - π(·)‖_TV ≤ Cρⁿ

where ‖·‖_TV denotes total variation distance.

Geometric ergodicity enables central limit theorems for MCMC estimators.

Theorem 4.5 (CLT for Geometrically Ergodic Chains). If {Xₙ} is geometrically ergodic with stationary distribution π and g has finite variance under π, then

√n(1/n ∑ᵢ₌₁ⁿ g(Xᵢ) - ∫ g dπ) →ᴰ N(0, σ²_∞)

where σ²_∞ = Var_π(g) + 2∑ₖ₌₁^∞ Cov_π(g(X₀), g(Xₖ))

The asymptotic variance σ²_∞ accounts for autocorrelation in the Markov chain, typically exceeding the i.i.d. variance Var_π(g).

4.7 Convergence Diagnostics

Practical MCMC implementation requires assessing when the chain has reached its stationary distribution and when sufficient samples have been collected for reliable inference.

4.7.1 Trace Plots and Visual Diagnostics

Trace plots of θ⁽ⁿ⁾ versus iteration n provide the first diagnostic tool. A well-mixing chain should exhibit: - Rapid movement throughout the parameter space - No obvious trends or drift - Consistent variability across iterations

"""
Generate trace plots for MCMC diagnostics
"""
function plot_traces(samples, parameter_names; burn_in=0)
    n_params = size(samples, 1)
    n_samples = size(samples, 2)
    
    plots = []
    for i in 1:n_params
        p = plot(1:n_samples, samples[i, :], 
                title=parameter_names[i],
                xlabel="Iteration", ylabel="Value",
                legend=false, linewidth=0.5)
        if burn_in > 0
            vline!([burn_in], color=:red, linestyle=:dash, linewidth=2)
        end
        push!(plots, p)
    end
    
    return plot(plots..., layout=(n_params, 1), size=(800, 200*n_params))
end

# Example using previous linear regression results
trace_plot = plot_traces(β_samples, ["β₁", "β₂", "β₃"], burn_in=1000)

4.7.2 Gelman-Rubin Diagnostic (R̂)

The potential scale reduction factor R̂ compares within-chain and between-chain variability to assess convergence.

Definition 4.8 (Gelman-Rubin R̂). For M parallel chains of length N each, R̂ is defined as:

R̂ = √((N-1)/N + M+1/MN · B/W)

where W is the within-chain variance and B is the between-chain variance.

Values R̂ > 1.1 suggest lack of convergence, while R̂ ≈ 1 indicates chains have mixed well.

"""
Compute Gelman-Rubin R-hat diagnostic
"""
function gelman_rubin(chains)
    M, N = size(chains)  # M chains, N samples each
    
    # Chain means and overall mean
    chain_means = mean(chains, dims=2)
    overall_mean = mean(chain_means)
    
    # Between-chain variance B
    B = N * sum((chain_means .- overall_mean).^2) / (M - 1)
    
    # Within-chain variance W
    W = sum(var(chains, dims=2)) / M
    
    # Potential scale reduction factor
    var_plus = (N - 1) / N * W + B / N
    R_hat = sqrt(var_plus / W)
    
    return R_hat
end

# Example: Generate multiple chains for comparison
function run_multiple_chains(log_target, initial_states, n_samples, proposal_sd)
    n_chains = length(initial_states)
    chains = Matrix{Float64}(undef, n_chains, n_samples)
    
    for i in 1:n_chains
        samples, _ = metropolis_hastings(log_target, initial_states[i], n_samples, proposal_sd)
        chains[i, :] = samples
    end
    
    return chains
end

# Run 4 chains with different starting points
initial_states = [-5.0, 0.0, 5.0, 10.0]
multiple_chains = run_multiple_chains(log_mixture_density, initial_states, 2000, 1.0)

# Compute R-hat after burn-in
burn_in = 500
R_hat = gelman_rubin(multiple_chains[:, burn_in+1:end])
println("R̂ = $(round(R_hat, digits=3))")

4.7.3 Effective Sample Size

The effective sample size (ESS) quantifies how many independent samples the MCMC chain is equivalent to, accounting for autocorrelation.

Definition 4.9 (Effective Sample Size). For a chain of length N, the ESS is:

ESS = N / (1 + 2∑ₖ₌₁^∞ ρₖ)

where ρₖ is the lag-k autocorrelation.

In practice, the sum is truncated when autocorrelations become negligible.

"""
Compute effective sample size using autocorrelation
"""
function effective_sample_size(x)
    N = length(x)
    x_centered = x .- mean(x)
    
    # Compute autocorrelations using FFT
    autocorr = real(ifft(abs2.(fft([x_centered; zeros(N)]))))[1:N]
    autocorr ./= autocorr[1]  # Normalize
    
    # Find cutoff where autocorrelation becomes negligible
    cutoff = findfirst(k -> autocorr[k] <= 0.05, 1:N)
    if cutoff === nothing
        cutoff = N
    end
    
    # Compute ESS
    sum_autocorr = 1 + 2 * sum(autocorr[2:cutoff])
    ess = N / sum_autocorr
    
    return max(ess, 1)  # ESS should be at least 1
end

# Compute ESS for the mixture example
ess_value = effective_sample_size(samples[1001:end])  # After burn-in
println("Effective sample size: $(round(ess_value, digits=1))")
println("Chain length: $(length(samples[1001:end]))")
println("Efficiency: $(round(ess_value/length(samples[1001:end]), digits=3))")

4.7.4 Autocorrelation Analysis

Understanding autocorrelation structure helps determine appropriate thinning intervals and assess chain efficiency.

"""
Compute and plot autocorrelation function
"""
function autocorrelation_plot(x, max_lag=50)
    N = length(x)
    max_lag = min(max_lag, N-1)
    
    x_centered = x .- mean(x)
    autocorr = zeros(max_lag + 1)
    
    for k in 0:max_lag
        if k == 0
            autocorr[k+1] = 1.0
        else
            autocorr[k+1] = cor(x_centered[1:N-k], x_centered[k+1:N])
        end
    end
    
    p = plot(0:max_lag, autocorr, 
             title="Autocorrelation Function",
             xlabel="Lag", ylabel="Autocorrelation",
             marker=:circle, legend=false)
    hline!([0], color=:black, linestyle=:dash)
    
    return p, autocorr
end

# Example autocorrelation analysis
autocorr_plot, autocorr_values = autocorrelation_plot(samples[1001:end])

# Find integrated autocorrelation time
integrated_time = 1 + 2 * sum(autocorr_values[2:end])
println("Integrated autocorrelation time: $(round(integrated_time, digits=2))")

4.8 Burn-in and Thinning

4.8.1 Burn-in Period

The burn-in period removes initial samples before the chain has converged to stationarity. Determining adequate burn-in length requires balancing computational efficiency with convergence assurance.

Common approaches include: 1. Fixed burn-in: Discard a predetermined fraction (e.g., first 25%) of samples 2. Convergence-based: Monitor diagnostics and stop burn-in when R̂ < 1.1 3. Visual assessment: Examine trace plots for apparent stationarity

4.8.2 Thinning

Thinning retains every k-th sample to reduce autocorrelation and storage requirements. While thinning can improve mixing diagnostics, it discards information and may not improve estimation efficiency.

Theorem 4.6 (Thinning and Efficiency). For estimating posterior means, thinning never improves asymptotic efficiency compared to using all samples.

Despite this theoretical result, thinning remains useful for: - Reducing storage requirements for long chains - Improving numerical stability of certain diagnostics - Creating approximately independent samples for downstream analyses

"""
Apply burn-in and thinning to MCMC samples
"""
function process_mcmc_samples(samples, burn_in_frac=0.25, thin=1)
    n_samples = length(samples)
    burn_in_length = Int(floor(burn_in_frac * n_samples))
    
    # Remove burn-in
    post_burnin = samples[burn_in_length+1:end]
    
    # Apply thinning
    if thin > 1
        thinned_indices = 1:thin:length(post_burnin)
        processed_samples = post_burnin[thinned_indices]
    else
        processed_samples = post_burnin
    end
    
    return processed_samples
end

# Example processing
processed = process_mcmc_samples(samples, 0.2, 5)
println("Original samples: $(length(samples))")
println("After burn-in (20%): $(length(samples) - Int(floor(0.2 * length(samples))))")
println("After thinning (every 5th): $(length(processed))")

4.9 Advanced Diagnostic Methods

4.9.1 Geweke Diagnostic

The Geweke diagnostic compares sample means from the beginning and end of the chain to detect trends or nonstationarity.

"""
Geweke convergence diagnostic
"""
function geweke_diagnostic(x, first_frac=0.1, last_frac=0.5)
    n = length(x)
    
    # First and last portions
    first_end = Int(floor(first_frac * n))
    last_start = Int(ceil((1 - last_frac) * n)) + 1
    
    x_first = x[1:first_end]
    x_last = x[last_start:end]
    
    # Sample means and standard errors (accounting for autocorrelation)
    mean_first = mean(x_first)
    mean_last = mean(x_last)
    
    # Simple standard errors (could be improved with spectral estimation)
    se_first = std(x_first) / sqrt(length(x_first))
    se_last = std(x_last) / sqrt(length(x_last))
    
    # Test statistic
    z_score = (mean_first - mean_last) / sqrt(se_first^2 + se_last^2)
    
    # Two-sided p-value
    p_value = 2 * (1 - cdf(Normal(0,


\newpage

# Chapter 5: Hamiltonian Monte Carlo and Modern Samplers

The random-walk Metropolis-Hastings algorithm, while theoretically sound and broadly applicable, suffers from fundamental limitations in high-dimensional spaces. As the dimension of the parameter space increases, the efficiency of random-walk proposals deteriorates dramatically due to the curse of dimensionality. Modern Bayesian computation demands more sophisticated sampling strategies that can exploit the geometric structure of the posterior distribution.

Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) represents a paradigm shift in MCMC methodology, replacing random perturbations with deterministic dynamics inspired by classical mechanics. By introducing auxiliary momentum variables and simulating Hamiltonian dynamics, HMC generates distant proposals that nonetheless maintain high acceptance probabilities. This chapter develops the theoretical foundations of HMC and its modern variants, culminating in the No-U-Turn Sampler (NUTS) that has become the default algorithm in contemporary Bayesian software.

## Hamiltonian Dynamics and Classical Mechanics

### The Hamiltonian Framework

Consider a system with generalized coordinates q ∈ ℝᵈ and corresponding momenta p ∈ ℝᵈ. The total energy of the system is described by the Hamiltonian function H(q, p), which governs the time evolution through Hamilton's equations:

> **Definition 5.1 (Hamilton's Equations).** For a Hamiltonian H(q, p), the dynamics of the system are given by:
> 
> dq/dt = ∂H/∂p
> 
> dp/dt = -∂H/∂p

These equations define a flow in the (q, p) phase space that preserves several crucial properties. Most importantly for MCMC applications, Hamiltonian dynamics preserve the total energy H(q, p) and conserve phase space volume.

> **Theorem 5.1 (Conservation of Energy).** If H(q, p) is time-independent, then H(q(t), p(t)) = H(q(0), p(0)) for all t.

*Proof.* Taking the total time derivative:

dH/dt = (∂H/∂q)ᵀ(dq/dt) + (∂H/∂p)ᵀ(dp/dt) = (∂H/∂q)ᵀ(∂H/∂p) + (∂H/∂p)ᵀ(-∂H/∂q) = 0

Thus H remains constant along trajectories. □

> **Theorem 5.2 (Volume Preservation).** The flow defined by Hamilton's equations preserves phase space volume (Liouville's theorem).

*Proof.* The Jacobian of the transformation (q₀, p₀) → (q(t), p(t)) has determinant 1. This follows from the fact that the divergence of the Hamiltonian vector field vanishes:

∇ · (∂H/∂p, -∂H/∂q) = tr(∂²H/∂q∂p) - tr(∂²H/∂p∂q) = 0

□

### Application to Bayesian Sampling

In the Bayesian context, we seek to sample from a posterior distribution π(θ) ∝ exp(-U(θ)) where U(θ) = -log π(θ) + constant is the negative log-posterior. We augment the parameter space by introducing auxiliary momentum variables p with the joint distribution:

π(θ, p) ∝ exp(-H(θ, p))

where the Hamiltonian takes the separable form:

H(θ, p) = U(θ) + K(p)

Here U(θ) represents the potential energy (negative log-posterior) and K(p) = ½pᵀM⁻¹p represents the kinetic energy, where M is a positive-definite mass matrix.

The marginal distribution of θ under this joint distribution is exactly our target posterior π(θ), while the momentum variables follow a multivariate normal distribution p ~ N(0, M). This construction allows us to simulate Hamiltonian dynamics in the augmented space and extract samples from the marginal posterior.

### The Leapfrog Integrator

Hamiltonian dynamics rarely admit analytical solutions, requiring numerical integration. The leapfrog integrator provides a symplectic (geometry-preserving) method that maintains the essential properties of the continuous dynamics.

> **Definition 5.2 (Leapfrog Integrator).** Given step size ε and current state (θ, p), one leapfrog step produces (θ', p') via:
> 
> p₁/₂ = p - (ε/2)∇U(θ)
> 
> θ' = θ + ε M⁻¹p₁/₂
> 
> p' = p₁/₂ - (ε/2)∇U(θ')

This three-stage update interleaves half-steps for momentum with full steps for position. The leapfrog integrator is time-reversible: applying the same update with step size -ε recovers the original state. It also exactly preserves volume in phase space, though it introduces small errors in energy conservation.

```julia
using LinearAlgebra, ForwardDiff, Distributions

function leapfrog_step(θ, p, grad_U, M_inv, ε)
    # Half step for momentum
    p_half = p - (ε/2) * grad_U(θ)
    
    # Full step for position
    θ_new = θ + ε * (M_inv * p_half)
    
    # Half step for momentum
    p_new = p_half - (ε/2) * grad_U(θ_new)
    
    return θ_new, p_new
end

# Example: Bivariate normal target
function neg_log_posterior(θ)
    # Target: N([0,0], I)
    return 0.5 * sum(θ.^2)
end

function grad_neg_log_posterior(θ)
    return θ
end

# Simulate trajectory
function simulate_trajectory(θ₀, p₀, L, ε, grad_U, M_inv)
    θ, p = θ₀, p₀
    trajectory = [(copy(θ), copy(p))]
    
    for i in 1:L
        θ, p = leapfrog_step(θ, p, grad_U, M_inv, ε)
        push!(trajectory, (copy(θ), copy(p)))
    end
    
    return trajectory
end

# Example trajectory
θ₀ = [1.5, -0.8]
p₀ = randn(2)
M_inv = I(2)  # Identity mass matrix
ε = 0.1
L = 20

trajectory = simulate_trajectory(θ₀, p₀, L, ε, grad_neg_log_posterior, M_inv)
println("Initial position: ", θ₀)
println("Final position: ", trajectory[end][1])
println("Energy change: ", sum(trajectory[1][1].^2)/2 + sum(trajectory[1][2].^2)/2 - 
                          sum(trajectory[end][1].^2)/2 - sum(trajectory[end][2].^2)/2)

4.10 Hamiltonian Monte Carlo Algorithm

4.10.1 Basic HMC Algorithm

The HMC algorithm combines Hamiltonian dynamics with a Metropolis acceptance step to ensure exact sampling from the target distribution despite discretization errors.

Algorithm 5.1 (Basic HMC). 1. Initialize: θ⁽⁰⁾, set iteration counter t = 0 2. For t = 1, 2, …, T: a. Sample momentum: p ~ N(0, M) b. Compute proposal: (θ, p) = LEAPFROG(θ⁽ᵗ⁻¹⁾, p, L, ε) c. Accept/reject: α = min(1, exp(H(θ⁽ᵗ⁻¹⁾, p) - H(θ, -p))) d. Update: θ⁽ᵗ⁾ = θ* with probability α, otherwise θ⁽ᵗ⁾ = θ⁽ᵗ⁻¹⁾

The momentum flip (-p*) in the acceptance probability ensures detailed balance. This arises because the leapfrog integrator is time-reversible, but we must account for the momentum direction to maintain the proper transition kernel.

Theorem 5.3 (Detailed Balance for HMC). The HMC transition kernel satisfies detailed balance with respect to the canonical distribution π(θ, p) ∝ exp(-H(θ, p)).

Proof. Consider the transition probability from (θ, p) to (θ’, p’). The proposal step is deterministic given the initial momentum, and the momentum is resampled from its invariant distribution. The acceptance probability ensures:

π(θ, p)P((θ, p) → (θ’, p’)) = π(θ’, p’)P((θ’, p’) → (θ, p))

by construction of the Metropolis-Hastings ratio. □

4.10.2 Performance Characteristics

HMC dramatically outperforms random-walk Metropolis in high dimensions due to its ability to generate distant proposals with high acceptance probability. The key insight is that Hamiltonian dynamics naturally follow the contours of the target distribution, avoiding the exponential degradation in acceptance rates that plague random-walk methods.

Theorem 5.4 (HMC Scaling). For a d-dimensional Gaussian target with condition number κ, optimal HMC achieves effective sample size per gradient evaluation that scales as O(κ^{1/2}) compared to O(κ) for random-walk Metropolis.

This represents a substantial improvement, particularly for ill-conditioned posteriors common in Bayesian hierarchical models.

4.10.3 Example: Multivariate Normal Target

using Turing, Distributions, LinearAlgebra, Random
Random.seed!(123)

# Define a challenging target: multivariate normal with correlation
function create_correlated_target(d, ρ)
    Σ = Matrix(I, d, d)
    for i in 1:d, j in 1:d
        if i ≠ j
            Σ[i,j] = ρ^abs(i-j)
        end
    end
    return MvNormal(zeros(d), Σ)
end

# HMC sampler implementation
mutable struct HMCSampler
    ε::Float64          # Step size
    L::Int              # Number of leapfrog steps
    M_inv::Matrix{Float64}  # Inverse mass matrix
    target_density::Function
    grad_log_density::Function
end

function hmc_step!(sampler, θ_current)
    d = length(θ_current)
    
    # Sample momentum
    p = rand(MvNormal(zeros(d), inv(sampler.M_inv)))
    
    # Store initial state
    θ_initial, p_initial = copy(θ_current), copy(p)
    H_initial = -sampler.target_density(θ_initial) + 0.5 * p'* sampler.M_inv * p
    
    # Simulate trajectory
    θ_prop, p_prop = θ_initial, p_initial
    for _ in 1:sampler.L
        θ_prop, p_prop = leapfrog_step(θ_prop, p_prop, 
                                       θ -> -sampler.grad_log_density(θ), 
                                       sampler.M_inv, sampler.ε)
    end
    
    # Compute final energy (with momentum flip)
    H_final = -sampler.target_density(θ_prop) + 0.5 * (-p_prop)' * sampler.M_inv * (-p_prop)
    
    # Accept/reject
    α = min(1.0, exp(H_initial - H_final))
    
    if rand() < α
        return θ_prop, true  # accepted
    else
        return θ_current, false  # rejected
    end
end

# Example: 10-dimensional correlated target
d = 10
ρ = 0.8
target = create_correlated_target(d, ρ)

log_density(θ) = logpdf(target, θ)
grad_log_density(θ) = inv(target.Σ) * (target.μ - θ)

# Initialize sampler
sampler = HMCSampler(0.15, 20, Matrix(I, d, d), log_density, grad_log_density)

# Run HMC
n_samples = 2000
samples = zeros(n_samples, d)
acceptances = Bool[]

θ = randn(d)
for i in 1:n_samples
    θ, accepted = hmc_step!(sampler, θ)
    samples[i, :] = θ
    push!(acceptances, accepted)
end

acceptance_rate = mean(acceptances)
println("HMC Acceptance Rate: ", round(acceptance_rate, digits=3))
println("Sample mean: ", round.(mean(samples, dims=1)[1, :], digits=3))
println("Target mean: ", target.μ)

# Effective sample size (simplified estimate)
function autocorrelation(x, max_lag=50)
    n = length(x)
    x = x .- mean(x)
    autocorr = zeros(max_lag + 1)
    
    for lag in 0:max_lag
        if lag == 0
            autocorr[lag + 1] = 1.0
        else
            autocorr[lag + 1] = sum(x[1:n-lag] .* x[lag+1:n]) / sum(x.^2)
        end
    end
    return autocorr
end

# Estimate effective sample size for first component
autocorr = autocorrelation(samples[:, 1])
# Find first negative autocorrelation
first_negative = findfirst(x -> x < 0, autocorr)
if first_negative === nothing
    first_negative = length(autocorr)
end

τ_int = 1 + 2 * sum(autocorr[2:first_negative-1])
eff_samples = n_samples / (2 * τ_int)
println("Integrated autocorr time: ", round(τ_int, digits=2))
println("Effective samples: ", round(eff_samples, digits=0))

4.11 Mass Matrix Adaptation

The choice of mass matrix M profoundly affects HMC performance. The optimal mass matrix is the inverse of the posterior covariance, M⁻¹ = Cov(θ|data), which makes the Hamiltonian dynamics isotropic in the transformed space.

4.11.1 Theoretical Motivation

Consider the second-order expansion of the negative log-posterior around its mode θ*:

U(θ) ≈ U(θ) + ½(θ - θ)ᵀG(θ - θ*)

where G is the Hessian matrix. For optimal performance, we want M⁻¹ = G, making the transformed dynamics:

H(θ, p) ≈ const + ½(θ - θ)ᵀG(θ - θ) + ½pᵀGp

This yields isotropic Gaussian dynamics in both position and momentum.

4.11.2 Adaptive Strategies

Algorithm 5.2 (Windowed Mass Matrix Adaptation). 1. Warmup phase: Run HMC for N_warmup iterations 2. For each adaptation window: a. Collect samples during the window b. Estimate sample covariance Σ̂ c. Update mass matrix: M = Σ̂ d. Reset sampler with new mass matrix 3. Sampling phase: Run HMC with final adapted mass matrix

using Statistics

function estimate_mass_matrix(samples; regularization=1e-6)
    """Estimate mass matrix from samples with regularization"""
    Σ = cov(samples)
    # Add regularization to diagonal for numerical stability
    Σ_reg = Σ + regularization * I(size(Σ, 1))
    return Σ_reg
end

function adaptive_hmc(target_density, grad_target, θ_init; 
                      n_warmup=1000, n_samples=2000, 
                      adaptation_windows=[200, 500, 800])
    
    d = length(θ_init)
    θ = copy(θ_init)
    
    # Initialize with identity mass matrix
    M = Matrix(I, d, d)
    ε = 0.1  # Will adapt this too
    L = 15
    
    all_samples = []
    
    # Warmup with adaptation
    println("Starting warmup with adaptation...")
    
    for (i, window_end) in enumerate(adaptation_windows)
        window_start = i == 1 ? 1 : adaptation_windows[i-1] + 1
        window_samples = zeros(window_end - window_start + 1, d)
        
        sampler = HMCSampler(ε, L, inv(M), target_density, grad_target)
        
        acceptances = Float64[]
        for j in 1:(window_end - window_start + 1)
            θ, accepted = hmc_step!(sampler, θ)
            window_samples[j, :] = θ
            push!(acceptances, float(accepted))
        end
        
        # Adapt step size based on acceptance rate
        current_accept_rate = mean(acceptances)
        if current_accept_rate > 0.65
            ε *= 1.1
        elseif current_accept_rate < 0.55
            ε *= 0.9
        end
        
        # Adapt mass matrix
        if size(window_samples, 1) > d + 1  # Ensure enough samples
            M = estimate_mass_matrix(window_samples)
        end
        
        append!(all_samples, [copy(window_samples[j, :]) for j in 1:size(window_samples, 1)])
        
        println("Window $i: Accept rate = $(round(current_accept_rate, digits=3)), ε = $(round(ε, digits=4))")
    end
    
    # Final sampling phase
    println("Starting final sampling...")
    sampler = HMCSampler(ε, L, inv(M), target_density, grad_target)
    final_samples = zeros(n_samples, d)
    
    for i in 1:n_samples
        θ, _ = hmc_step!(sampler, θ)
        final_samples[i, :] = θ
    end
    
    return final_samples, M
end

# Test adaptive HMC on challenging target
target = create_correlated_target(5, 0.9)  # High correlation
θ_init = randn(5)

final_samples, adapted_M = adaptive_hmc(
    θ -> logpdf(target, θ),
    θ -> inv(target.Σ) * (target.μ - θ),
    θ_init
)

println("True covariance (first 3x3 block):")
display(target.Σ[1:3, 1:3])
println("Adapted mass matrix (first 3x3 block):")
display(adapted_M[1:3, 1:3])
println("Sample covariance (first 3x3 block):")
display(cov(final_samples)[1:3, 1:3])

4.12 No-U-Turn Sampler (NUTS)

The primary limitation of basic HMC lies in the need to specify the trajectory length L. Too short trajectories fail to explore efficiently, while too long trajectories waste computation by doubling back toward the starting point. The No-U-Turn Sampler addresses this by automatically determining trajectory length.

4.12.1 The U-Turn Criterion

A trajectory makes a “U-turn” when it begins to return toward its starting region. Formally, this occurs when the velocity vector p becomes negatively correlated with the displacement from the starting point.

Definition 5.3 (U-Turn Criterion). Given starting position θ₀ and current state (θ, p) on a trajectory, a U-turn occurs when:

(θ - θ₀) · p < 0

This criterion detects when the trajectory begins curving back, indicating that further simulation provides diminishing returns.

4.12.2 NUTS Algorithm

The NUTS algorithm builds trajectories incrementally, doubling the length at each step until a U-turn is detected or a maximum tree depth is reached.

Algorithm 5.3 (NUTS - Simplified Version). 1. Initialize: θ₀, sample p₀ ~ N(0, M) 2. Set: j = 0, θ⁻ = θ⁺ = θ₀, p⁻ = p⁺ = p₀ 3. While no U-turn and j < max_depth: a. Choose direction: v ~ Uniform({-1, +1}) b. If v = 1: extend forward from (θ⁺, p⁺) c. If v = -1: extend backward from (θ⁻, p⁻) d. Build subtree of length 2ʲ in chosen direction e. Check U-turn criterion between endpoints f. Sample candidate from all states in combined tree g. Increment: j = j + 1 4. Return sampled candidate

The algorithm maintains detailed balance by using a clever sampling scheme from the binary tree of states, weighting by the relative probabilities (energies) of states.

4.12.3 Efficient Implementation

using Random

mutable struct NUTSState
    θ::Vector{Float64}
    p::Vector{Float64}
    energy::Float64
    grad::Vector{Float64}
end

function compute_energy(state, target_density, M_inv)
    potential = -target_density(state.θ)
    kinetic = 0.5 * state.p' * M_inv * state.p
    return potential + kinetic
end

function build_tree(state, direction, depth, ε, target_density, grad_target, M_inv)
    """Recursively build tree of given depth in specified direction"""
    
    if depth == 0
        # Base case: single leapfrog step
        θ_new, p_new = leapfrog_step(state.θ, state.p, 
                                     θ -> -grad_target(θ), M_inv, 
                                     direction * ε)
        
        new_state = NUTSState(θ_new, p_new, 0.0, grad_target(θ_new))
        new_state.energy = compute_energy(new_state, target_density, M_inv)
        
        # Check if proposal is valid (not infinite energy)
        valid = isfinite(new_state.energy)
        
        # For single step, no subtree statistics needed
        return new_state, new_state, new_state, valid, false, 1, 0.0
    else
        # Recursive case: build left and right subtrees
        
        # Build first half-tree
        θ_minus, p_minus, θ_plus, p_plus, proposal_state, valid_left, u_turn_left, 
        n_left, sum_log_prob_left = build_tree(state, direction, depth - 1, 
                                               ε, target_density, grad_target, M_inv)
        
        if valid_left && !u_turn_left
            # Build second half-tree
            if direction == 1
                start_state = NUTSState(θ_plus, p_plus, 0.0, grad_target(θ_plus))
            else
                start_state = NUTSState(θ_minus, p_minus, 0.0, grad_target(θ_minus))
            end
            
            θ_minus_2, p_minus_2, θ_plus_2, p_plus_2, proposal_state_2, 
            valid_right, u_turn_right, n_right, sum_log_prob_right = 
                build_tree(start_state, direction, depth - 1, 
                           ε, target_density, grad_target, M_inv)
            
            # Update endpoints
            if direction == 1
                θ_plus, p_plus = θ_plus_2, p_plus_2
            else
                θ_minus, p_minus = θ_minus_2, p_minus_2
            end
            
            # Check for U-turn across the full tree
            u_turn = u_turn_left || u_turn_right || 
                     ((θ_plus - θ_minus)' * p_minus < 0) ||
                     ((θ_plus - θ_minus)' * p_plus < 0)
            
            valid = valid_left && valid_right
            
            # Metropolis sampling from the two subtrees
            n_total = n_left + n_right
            sum_log_prob_total = sum_log_prob_left + sum_log_prob_right
            
            if valid && n_total > 0
                prob_right = n_right / n_total
                if rand() < prob_right
                    proposal_state = proposal_state_2
                end
            end
            
            return θ_minus, p_minus, θ_plus, p_plus, proposal_state, 
                   valid, u_turn, n_total, sum_log_prob_total
        else
            # Left subtree invalid or made U-turn
            return θ_minus, p_minus, θ_plus, p_plus, proposal_state, 
                   valid_left, true, n_left, sum_log_prob_left
        end
    end
end

function nuts_step(θ_current, ε, max_depth, target_density, grad_target, M_inv)
    """Single NUTS step"""
    
    d = length(θ_current)
    
    # Sample initial momentum
    p₀ = rand(MvNormal(zeros(d), inv(M_inv)))
    
    # Initialize
    state₀ = NUTSState(copy(θ_current), copy(p₀), 0.0, grad_target(θ_current))
    state₀.energy = compute_energy(state₀, target_density, M_inv)
    
    θ_minus = copy(θ_current)
    θ_plus = copy(θ_current)
    p_minus = copy(p₀)
    p_plus = copy(p₀)
    
    proposal_state = state₀
    depth = 0
    
    while depth < max_depth
        # Choose direction randomly
        direction = rand([-1, 1])
        
        # Build tree in chosen direction
        if direction == 1
            start_state = NUTSState(θ_plus, p_plus, 0.0, grad_target(θ_plus))
            _, _, θ_plus, p_plus, candidate, valid, u_turn, n, _ = 
                build_tree(start_state, direction, depth, ε, target_density, grad_target, M_inv)
        else
            start_state = NUTSState(θ_minus, p_minus, 0.0, grad_target(θ_minus))
            θ_minus, p_minus, _, _, candidate, valid, u_turn, n, _ = 
                build_tree(start_state, direction, depth, ε, target_density, grad_target, M_inv)
        end
        
        # Accept candidate with appropriate probability
        if valid && !u_turn && n > 0
            accept_prob = min(1.0, n)  # Simplified for this example
            if rand() < accept_prob
                proposal_state = candidate
            end
        end
        
        # Check stopping criteria
        if u_turn || !valid
            break
        end
        
        # Check global U-turn condition
        if (θ_plus - θ_minus)' * p_minus < 0 || (θ_plus - θ_minus)' * p_plus < 0
            break
        end
        
        depth += 1
    end
    
    return proposal_state.θ, depth
end

# Example usage
println("Testing NUTS sampler...")

target = MvNormal([0.0, 0.0], [1.0 0.8; 0.8 1.0])
n_samples = 1000
θ_init = [0.0, 0.0]
ε = 0.1
max_depth = 10

samples = zeros(n_samples, 2)
tree_depths = Int[]

θ = copy(θ_init)
for i in 1:n_samples
    θ, depth = nuts_step(θ, ε, max_depth, 
                        x -> logpdf(target, x),
                        x -> inv(target.Σ) * (target.μ - x),
                        Matrix(I, 2, 2))
    samples[i, :] = θ
    push!(tree_depths, depth)
end

println("Average tree depth: ", round(mean(tree_depths), digits=2))
println("Sample mean: ", round.(mean(samples, dims=1)[1, :], digits=3))
println("Sample covariance:")
display(round.(cov(samples), digits=3))
println("Target covariance:")
display(target.Σ)

4.13 Riemannian HMC

For challenging posterior geometries with varying curvature, standard HMC with a fixed mass matrix may still perform poorly. Riemannian HMC generalizes the approach by allowing the mass matrix to vary across the parameter space, following the local geometry of the posterior.

4.13.1 Riemannian Geometry and Metric Tensors

In Riemannian HMC, we replace the constant mass matrix M with a position-dependent metric tensor G(θ). The kinetic energy becomes:

K(θ, p) = ½pᵀG(θ)⁻¹p

This modification requires updating Hamilton’s equations to account for the position-dependent metric:

dθ/dt = G(θ)⁻¹p

dp/dt = -∇U(θ) - ½∑ᵢpᵢ∇G(θ)ᵢⱼG(θ)⁻¹p

The additional “geometric force” term -½∑ᵢpᵢ∇G(θ)ᵢⱼG(θ)⁻¹p accounts for the changing geometry.

4.13.2 Choice of Metric Tensor

The optimal choice for G(θ) is the Fisher information matrix:

G(θ) = E[∇log π(θ|data)∇log π(θ|data)ᵀ | θ]

For many models, this equals the negative Hessian of the log-posterior (by the information matrix equality):

G(θ) = -∇²log π(θ|data)

This choice makes the transformed dynamics locally isotropic, adapting automatically to the posterior’s curvature.

4.13.3 Implementation Challenges

Riemannian HMC requires computing matrix inversions and derivatives at each step, making it computationally intensive. Modern implementations use approximations such as:

  1. Block-diagonal approximations to G(θ)
  2. Stochastic approximations using minibatches
  3. Implicit integrators for numerical stability

4.14 Comparison with Random-Walk Metropolis

The fundamental advantage of HMC over random-walk Metropolis becomes apparent in high-dimensional problems. Consider a d-dimensional Gaussian target with condition number κ.

4.14.1 Scaling Properties

Theorem 5.5 (Optimal Scaling for Random-Walk Metropolis). For a d-dimensional Gaussian target, the optimal acceptance rate approaches 23.4% as d → ∞, and the asymptotic efficiency scales as O(d⁻¹).

Proof sketch. The result follows from analyzing the asymptotic efficiency of the Langevin diffusion approximation as the proposal variance is optimally tuned. □

In contrast, HMC maintains bounded efficiency as dimension increases:

**Theorem 5.6 (H

5 Chapter 6: Variational Inference

Variational inference transforms the intractable problem of posterior integration into an optimization problem over a family of tractable distributions. By casting inference as optimization, variational methods enable scalable approximate Bayesian computation for complex models where MCMC methods become prohibitively expensive. This chapter develops the theoretical foundations of variational inference, establishes the evidence lower bound as the central optimization objective, and presents both classical coordinate ascent and modern stochastic methods for variational optimization.

5.1 6.1 The Variational Principle and Evidence Lower Bound

The fundamental challenge in Bayesian inference is computing the posterior distribution p(θ|x) = p(x|θ)p(θ)/p(x), where the marginal likelihood p(x) = ∫ p(x|θ)p(θ)dθ is often intractable. Variational inference sidesteps this integration by approximating the posterior with a simpler distribution from a tractable family.

Definition 6.1 (Variational Family). A variational family Q is a collection of probability distributions {q(θ; λ) : λ ∈ Λ} parameterized by variational parameters λ, where each q(θ; λ) is normalized and from which we can efficiently sample and evaluate densities.

The variational inference problem seeks the member of Q closest to the true posterior p(θ|x) under the Kullback-Leibler divergence:

λ* = arg min_{λ} KL(q(θ; λ) || p(θ|x))

Since KL(q || p) = E_q[log q(θ)] - E_q[log p(θ|x)], this minimization is equivalent to maximizing E_q[log p(θ|x)]. However, this expectation still involves the intractable posterior. The key insight is to work with a tractable lower bound.

Theorem 6.1 (Evidence Lower Bound). For any distribution q(θ) and observed data x, the log marginal likelihood satisfies:

log p(x) ≥ E_q[log p(x,θ)] - E_q[log q(θ)] =: L(q)

with equality if and only if q(θ) = p(θ|x).

Proof. We have:

log p(x) = log ∫ p(x,θ) dθ
         = log ∫ p(x,θ) (q(θ)/q(θ)) dθ
         = log E_q[p(x,θ)/q(θ)]
         ≥ E_q[log(p(x,θ)/q(θ))]    (Jensen's inequality)
         = E_q[log p(x,θ)] - E_q[log q(θ)] = L(q)

Equality holds when p(x,θ)/q(θ) is constant almost surely under q, which occurs when q(θ) ∝ p(x,θ), i.e., q(θ) = p(θ|x). □

The evidence lower bound (ELBO) L(q) provides our optimization objective. Since log p(x) is constant with respect to q, maximizing L(q) is equivalent to minimizing KL(q || p(θ|x)). We can rewrite the ELBO as:

L(q) = E_q[log p(x|θ)] - KL(q(θ) || p(θ))

This decomposition reveals the bias-variance tradeoff inherent in variational approximation: the first term encourages q to place mass where the likelihood is large, while the second term penalizes deviation from the prior, preventing overfitting to the data.

5.2 6.2 Mean-Field Variational Families

The choice of variational family Q determines both the quality of the approximation and the computational complexity of optimization. The most common choice is the mean-field family, which assumes independence among groups of parameters.

Definition 6.2 (Mean-Field Variational Family). A mean-field variational family with partition θ = (θ₁, …, θₖ) assumes:

q(θ) = ∏ᵢ₌₁ᵏ qᵢ(θᵢ; λᵢ)

where each factor qᵢ is typically chosen from an exponential family.

While mean-field approximations ignore posterior correlations, they often provide reasonable approximations for many models and enable efficient optimization through coordinate ascent.

Theorem 6.2 (Mean-Field ELBO Decomposition). Under the mean-field assumption q(θ) = ∏ᵢ qᵢ(θᵢ), the ELBO decomposes as:

L(q) = E_q[log p(x, θ)] - ∑ᵢ E_qᵢ[log qᵢ(θᵢ)]

Proof. Direct substitution yields:

L(q) = E_q[log p(x,θ)] - E_q[log q(θ)]
     = E_q[log p(x,θ)] - E_q[∑ᵢ log qᵢ(θᵢ)]
     = E_q[log p(x,θ)] - ∑ᵢ E_qᵢ[log qᵢ(θᵢ)]

where the last equality follows from the independence assumption. □

5.3 6.3 Coordinate Ascent Variational Inference

Coordinate ascent variational inference (CAVI) optimizes the ELBO by iteratively updating each factor qⱼ(θⱼ) while holding all other factors fixed. This approach is guaranteed to increase the ELBO at each step and converge to a local maximum.

Theorem 6.3 (CAVI Update Equations). The optimal update for factor qⱼ(θⱼ) in mean-field variational inference is:

qⱼ*(θⱼ) ∝ exp(E_{q₋ⱼ}[log p(x, θ)])

where q₋ⱼ denotes the product of all factors except qⱼ.

Proof. We maximize L(q) with respect to qⱼ subject to the normalization constraint. Using Lagrange multipliers:

∇_qⱼ [E_q[log p(x,θ)] - E_qⱼ[log qⱼ(θⱼ)] - λ(∫ qⱼ(θⱼ)dθⱼ - 1)] = 0

This yields: E_{q₋ⱼ}[log p(x,θ)] - log qⱼ(θⱼ) - 1 - λ = 0

Therefore: qⱼ*(θⱼ) ∝ exp(E_{q₋ⱼ}[log p(x,θ)]). □

5.3.1 Example 6.1: Bayesian Linear Regression

Consider the Bayesian linear regression model: - y ~ N(Xβ, σ²I)
- β ~ N(0, τ²I) - σ² ~ InvGamma(a, b)

We approximate the posterior p(β, σ²|y, X) using mean-field with q(β)q(σ²).

For the precision parameter σ⁻² = γ, taking q_γ ~ Gamma(α, β_γ) and q_β ~ N(μ_β, Σ_β), the CAVI updates become:

β update: E_{q_γ}[log p(y, β, γ|X)] = -½E_γ[(y - Xβ)ᵀ(y - Xβ)] - ½τ⁻²βᵀβ + const

This yields: - Σ_β = (τ⁻²I + E_γ[γ]XᵀX)⁻¹ - μ_β = E_γ[γ]Σ_β XᵀY

γ update: E_{q_β}[log p(y, β, γ|X)] = -½γE_β[(y - Xβ)ᵀ(y - Xβ)] + (a-1)log γ - bγ + const

This gives: - α = a + n/2
- β_γ = b + ½E_β[(y - Xβ)ᵀ(y - Xβ)]

where E_β[(y - Xβ)ᵀ(y - Xβ)] = (y - Xμ_β)ᵀ(y - Xμ_β) + tr(XᵀXΣ_β).

using Distributions, LinearAlgebra, Random

function cavi_linear_regression(X, y; τ²=1.0, a=1.0, b=1.0, max_iter=100, tol=1e-6)
    n, p = size(X)
    
    # Initialize variational parameters
    μ_β = zeros(p)
    Σ_β = I(p)
    α = a + n/2
    β_γ = b + 1.0
    
    elbo_vals = Float64[]
    
    for iter in 1:max_iter
        # Update β parameters
        E_γ = α / β_γ
        Σ_β = inv(1/τ² * I(p) + E_γ * X'X)
        μ_β = E_γ * Σ_β * X'y
        
        # Update γ parameters  
        residual_ss = (y - X*μ_β)'*(y - X*μ_β) + tr(X'X * Σ_β)
        β_γ = b + 0.5 * residual_ss
        
        # Compute ELBO
        elbo = compute_elbo_linear(X, y, μ_β, Σ_β, α, β_γ, τ², a, b)
        push!(elbo_vals, elbo)
        
        # Check convergence
        if iter > 1 && abs(elbo_vals[end] - elbo_vals[end-1]) < tol
            break
        end
    end
    
    return μ_β, Σ_β, α, β_γ, elbo_vals
end

function compute_elbo_linear(X, y, μ_β, Σ_β, α, β_γ, τ², a, b)
    n, p = size(X)
    E_γ = α / β_γ
    E_log_γ = digamma(α) - log(β_γ)
    
    # E[log p(y|β,γ,X)]
    term1 = 0.5*n*E_log_γ - 0.5*E_γ*((y - X*μ_β)'*(y - X*μ_β) + tr(X'X * Σ_β))
    
    # E[log p(β)]  
    term2 = -0.5*p*log(2π*τ²) - 1/(2*τ²)*(μ_β'*μ_β + tr(Σ_β))
    
    # E[log p(γ)]
    term3 = a*log(b) - loggamma(a) + (a-1)*E_log_γ - b*E_γ
    
    # H[q(β)]
    term4 = 0.5*logdet(2π*ℯ*Σ_β)
    
    # H[q(γ)]  
    term5 = α - log(β_γ) + loggamma(α) + (1-α)*digamma(α)
    
    return term1 + term2 + term3 + term4 + term5
end

5.4 6.4 Stochastic Variational Inference

CAVI requires computing expectations over the entire dataset at each iteration, making it impractical for large datasets. Stochastic variational inference (SVI) addresses this by using noisy gradients computed on mini-batches, enabling scalable inference.

The key insight is that many models have a natural factorization p(x, θ) = p(θ)∏ᵢ p(xᵢ|θ), where the ELBO becomes:

L(q) = E_q[log p(θ)] - E_q[log q(θ)] + ∑ᵢ E_q[log p(xᵢ|θ)]

For a mini-batch of size B from N total observations, we obtain an unbiased estimator:

L̃(q) = E_q[log p(θ)] - E_q[log q(θ)] + (N/B) ∑_{i ∈ batch} E_q[log p(xᵢ|θ)]

Definition 6.3 (Natural Gradients). For exponential family variational distributions q(θ; λ) = h(θ)exp(λᵀt(θ) - A(λ)), the natural gradient of the ELBO is:

∇̃_λ L(q) = E_q[∇_λ log q(θ; λ)] (∇_λ L(q))

Natural gradients account for the geometry of the parameter space, often leading to faster convergence than ordinary gradients.

5.4.1 Example 6.2: Stochastic Variational Inference for Bayesian Logistic Regression

Consider logistic regression with y ∈ {0,1}ⁿ and prior β ~ N(0, σ²I). We use q(β) = N(μ, S) and optimize via natural gradients.

using ForwardDiff, Optimization, OptimizationOptimJL

function svi_logistic_regression(X, y; σ²=1.0, batch_size=32, max_iter=1000, lr=0.01)
    n, p = size(X)
    
    # Initialize variational parameters (natural parameterization)
    λ₁ = zeros(p)  # μ/σ²  
    λ₂ = -0.5 * ones(p)  # -1/(2σ²)
    
    function elbo_estimate(params, batch_idx)
        λ₁, λ₂ = params[1:p], params[p+1:2p]
        
        # Convert to mean parameterization
        Σ = -0.5 ./ λ₂
        μ = λ₁ .* Σ
        
        # Prior contribution
        prior_term = -0.5 * sum(μ.^2 .+ Σ) / σ² - 0.5*p*log(2π*σ²)
        
        # Entropy term  
        entropy = 0.5 * sum(log.(2π*ℯ*Σ))
        
        # Likelihood term (mini-batch)
        X_batch = X[batch_idx, :]
        y_batch = y[batch_idx]
        
        likelihood = 0.0
        for (xi, yi) in zip(eachrow(X_batch), y_batch)
            # Use Gaussian approximation for intractable likelihood integral
            m = dot(xi, μ)
            s² = dot(xi.^2, Σ)
            approx_logit = m / sqrt(1 + π*s²/8)
            likelihood += yi * approx_logit - log(1 + exp(approx_logit))
        end
        
        # Scale likelihood by N/B
        likelihood *= n / length(batch_idx)
        
        return prior_term + entropy + likelihood
    end
    
    # Stochastic optimization
    params = vcat(λ₁, λ₂)
    elbo_vals = Float64[]
    
    for iter in 1:max_iter
        # Sample mini-batch
        batch_idx = sample(1:n, batch_size, replace=false)
        
        # Compute gradient
        grad = ForwardDiff.gradient(p -> -elbo_estimate(p, batch_idx), params)
        
        # Natural gradient step (simplified)
        params .- = lr * grad
        
        # Monitor ELBO on full dataset every 50 iterations
        if iter % 50 == 0
            full_elbo = elbo_estimate(params, 1:n)
            push!(elbo_vals, full_elbo)
        end
    end
    
    # Convert back to mean parameterization
    λ₁, λ₂ = params[1:p], params[p+1:2p]
    Σ = -0.5 ./ λ₂  
    μ = λ₁ .* Σ
    
    return μ, Σ, elbo_vals
end

5.5 6.5 Black-Box Variational Inference

When model-specific CAVI updates are intractable, black-box variational inference (BBVI) treats the model as an oracle that can evaluate log p(x,θ) and its gradients. BBVI optimizes the ELBO using two main gradient estimation techniques: the reparameterization trick and the score function estimator.

5.5.1 6.5.1 Reparameterization Trick

For many distributions, we can express q(θ; λ) = f(ε; λ) where ε ~ p(ε) is auxiliary noise independent of λ. This reparameterization enables low-variance gradient estimation.

Theorem 6.4 (Reparameterization Gradient). If θ = f(ε; λ) with ε ~ p(ε), then:

∇_λ E_q[h(θ)] = E_ε[∇_λ h(f(ε; λ))]

Proof. Using the change of variables θ = f(ε; λ):

∇_λ E_q[h(θ)] = ∇_λ ∫ h(θ) q(θ; λ) dθ
               = ∇_λ ∫ h(f(ε; λ)) p(ε) dε
               = ∫ ∇_λ h(f(ε; λ)) p(ε) dε    (interchange differentiation and integration)
               = E_ε[∇_λ h(f(ε; λ))]

□

The reparameterization trick applies directly to location-scale families. For q(θ) = N(μ, σ²), we use θ = μ + σε with ε ~ N(0,1).

5.5.2 6.5.2 Score Function Estimator

When reparameterization is unavailable, the score function (REINFORCE) estimator provides a general gradient estimation method.

Theorem 6.5 (Score Function Gradient). For any q(θ; λ), the gradient satisfies:

∇_λ E_q[h(θ)] = E_q[h(θ) ∇_λ log q(θ; λ)]

Proof.

∇_λ E_q[h(θ)] = ∇_λ ∫ h(θ) q(θ; λ) dθ
               = ∫ h(θ) ∇_λ q(θ; λ) dθ
               = ∫ h(θ) q(θ; λ) ∇_λ log q(θ; λ) dθ
               = E_q[h(θ) ∇_λ log q(θ; λ)]

□

The score function estimator has higher variance than reparameterization but applies to discrete distributions and complex continuous distributions without available reparameterizations.

5.5.3 Example 6.3: Black-Box VI for Mixture Model

using Distributions, Zygote

function bbvi_gaussian_mixture(x; K=2, max_iter=1000, lr=0.01)
    n = length(x)
    
    # Variational parameters: mixture weights (logits) and component parameters
    π_logits = randn(K-1)  # K-1 logits for K-simplex
    μ_means = randn(K)
    μ_vars = ones(K)
    σ_shapes = ones(K) * 2.0
    σ_rates = ones(K) * 1.0
    
    function elbo_estimate(π_logits, μ_means, μ_vars, σ_shapes, σ_rates; n_samples=10)
        # Convert to probabilities
        π_probs = softmax([π_logits; 0.0])  # Add 0 for last component
        
        elbo = 0.0
        
        for _ in 1:n_samples
            # Sample component parameters
            μ_samples = [randn() * sqrt(μ_vars[k]) + μ_means[k] for k in 1:K]
            σ_samples = [rand(Gamma(σ_shapes[k], 1/σ_rates[k])) for k in 1:K]
            
            # Compute log-likelihood
            log_like = sum(log(sum(π_probs[k] * pdf(Normal(μ_samples[k], sqrt(σ_samples[k])), xi) 
                                  for k in 1:K)) for xi in x)
            
            # Prior terms
            log_prior_μ = sum(-0.5 * μ_samples[k]^2 for k in 1:K)  # N(0,1) priors
            log_prior_σ = sum(-σ_samples[k] for k in 1:K)  # Exp(1) priors  
            log_prior_π = 0.0  # Uniform prior
            
            # Entropy terms (analytical for exponential families)
            entropy_μ = 0.5 * sum(log(2π*ℯ*μ_vars[k]) for k in 1:K)
            entropy_σ = sum(σ_shapes[k] - log(σ_rates[k]) + loggamma(σ_shapes[k]) + 
                           (1-σ_shapes[k])*digamma(σ_shapes[k]) for k in 1:K)
            entropy_π = -sum(π_probs[k] * log(max(π_probs[k], 1e-10)) for k in 1:K)
            
            elbo += log_like + log_prior_μ + log_prior_σ + log_prior_π + 
                   entropy_μ + entropy_σ + entropy_π
        end
        
        return elbo / n_samples
    end
    
    # Optimization via automatic differentiation
    params = (π_logits, μ_means, μ_vars, σ_shapes, σ_rates)
    elbo_vals = Float64[]
    
    for iter in 1:max_iter
        elbo, grads = Zygote.withgradient(p -> elbo_estimate(p...), params)
        
        # Gradient ascent step
        params = map((p, g) -> p + lr * g, params, grads[1])
        
        # Ensure positivity constraints
        μ_vars = max.(params[3], 1e-6)
        σ_shapes = max.(params[4], 1e-6) 
        σ_rates = max.(params[5], 1e-6)
        params = (params[1], params[2], μ_vars, σ_shapes, σ_rates)
        
        if iter % 100 == 0
            push!(elbo_vals, elbo)
            println("Iteration $iter: ELBO = $elbo")
        end
    end
    
    return params, elbo_vals
end

# Helper function
softmax(x) = exp.(x) ./ sum(exp.(x))

5.6 6.6 Normalizing Flows for Flexible Variational Families

Mean-field approximations often fail to capture important posterior correlations. Normalizing flows construct flexible variational families by transforming simple base distributions through invertible neural networks.

Definition 6.4 (Normalizing Flow). A normalizing flow is a sequence of invertible transformations f₁, …, fₖ such that if z₀ ~ q₀(z₀), then zₖ = fₖ ∘ … ∘ f₁(z₀) has density:

q(zₖ) = q₀(z₀) ∏ᵢ₌₁ᵏ |det J_fᵢ(zᵢ₋₁)|⁻¹

where J_fᵢ is the Jacobian of fᵢ.

Popular flow architectures include: - Planar flows: f(z) = z + uh(wᵀz + b) where h is smooth - Radial flows: f(z) = z + βh(α, r)(z - z₀) with r = ||z - z₀|| - Real NVP: Partition z = [z₁, z₂] and use f(z) = [z₁, z₂ ⊙ exp(s(z₁)) + t(z₁)]

The variational objective becomes: L(φ) = E_z₀[log p(fφ(z₀)) - log q₀(z₀) - ∑ᵢ log |det J_fᵢ(zᵢ₋₁)|]

5.6.1 Example 6.4: Planar Flow Implementation

using Flux, Zygote, LinearAlgebra

struct PlanarFlow
    u::Vector{Float64}
    w::Vector{Float64} 
    b::Float64
end

function (flow::PlanarFlow)(z)
    h_prime = tanh
    h = x -> log(cosh(x))  # Antiderivative of tanh
    
    wz_b = dot(flow.w, z) + flow.b
    z_new = z + flow.u * h_prime(wz_b)
    
    # Compute log |det J|
    psi = h_prime'(wz_b) * flow.w  # h'(w^T z + b) * w
    log_det_J = log(abs(1 + dot(flow.u, psi)))
    
    return z_new, log_det_J
end

function train_planar_flow_vi(log_target_density, dim; n_flows=5, n_samples=1000, 
                             max_iter=2000, lr=0.01)
    # Initialize flow parameters
    flows = [PlanarFlow(randn(dim), randn(dim), randn()) for _ in 1:n_flows]
    
    function elbo_estimate(flows)
        # Sample from base distribution
        z₀ = randn(dim, n_samples)
        log_q₀ = -0.5 * sum(z₀.^2, dims=1)[:] .- 0.5 * dim * log(2π)
        
        # Forward pass through flows
        z = copy(z₀)
        log_det_sum = zeros(n_samples)
        
        for flow in flows
            z_new = similar(z)
            for i in 1:n_samples
                z_new[:, i], log_det = flow(z[:, i])
                log_det_sum[i] += log_det
            end
            z = z_new
        end
        
        # Compute target log density
        log_target = [log_target_density(z[:, i]) for i in 1:n_samples]
        
        # ELBO estimate
        return mean(log_target + log_det_sum - log_q₀)
    end
    
    # Training loop with Zygote
    elbo_vals = Float64[]
    
    for iter in 1:max_iter
        elbo, grads = Zygote.withgradient(elbo_estimate, flows)
        
        # Update parameters
        for (i, grad) in enumerate(grads[1])
            flows[i].u .-= lr * grad.u
            flows[i].w .-= lr * grad.w  
            flows[i].b -= lr * grad.b
        end
        
        if iter % 200 == 0
            push!(elbo_vals, elbo)
            println("Iteration $iter: ELBO = $elbo")
        end
    end
    
    return flows, elbo_vals
end

5.7 6.7 Comparison with MCMC: Speed vs. Accuracy Tradeoff

Variational inference and MCMC represent fundamentally different approaches to approximate Bayesian computation, with distinct advantages and limitations.

5.7.1 6.7.1 Computational Complexity

Variational Inference: - Time complexity: O(T × cost_per_iteration) where T is iterations to convergence - Memory complexity: O(|λ|) for variational parameters - Highly parallelizable across data points and parameters - Convergence typically requires 10²-10³ iterations

MCMC:
- Time complexity: O(S × cost_per_sample) where S includes burn-in and thinning - Memory complexity: O(S × |θ|) for sample storage - Sequential sampling limits parallelization - Effective samples often require 10³-10⁵ iterations

For large datasets, VI’s ability to subsample data provides significant computational advantages.

5.7.2 6.7.2 Approximation Quality

Variational Inference: - Provides lower bound on marginal likelihood - Systematically underestimates posterior uncertainty - Mode-seeking behavior: finds modes but may miss multi-modality - Approximation quality limited by variational family expressiveness

MCMC: - Asymptotically exact (given sufficient mixing) - Provides unbiased estimates of posterior expectations - Can explore multi-modal distributions effectively
- Quality depends on mixing and chain length

Theorem 6.6 (VI Underestimation of Uncertainty). For any variational approximation q*(θ) = arg min_q KL(q(θ) || p(θ|x)), the posterior variance satisfies:

Var_q*[θ] ≤ Var_p[θ|x]

Proof. The reverse KL divergence KL(q || p) = ∫ q(θ) log(q(θ)/p(θ|x)) dθ penalizes q for having support where p has little mass. This mode-seeking behavior causes q* to concentrate mass more than p, reducing variance. □

5.7.3 6.7.3 Practical Guidelines

Use Variational Inference when: - Dataset size exceeds computational capacity for MCMC - Real-time or interactive inference is required - Model has natural conjugacy structure - Point estimates and uncertainty quantification suffice

Use MCMC when: - High-fidelity posterior approximation is critical - Model exhibits strong multi-modality - Computational budget allows sufficient sampling - Exact posterior moments and quantiles are needed

5.8 6.8 Pathologies of Variational Inference

While variational inference provides computational advantages, several pathological behaviors can compromise inference quality.

5.8.1 6.8.1 Mode Collapse

Mean-field VI systematically underestimates posterior correlations and can collapse to single modes in multi-modal posteriors.

5.8.2 6.8.2 Local Optima

The ELBO is non-convex in general, leading to sensitivity to initialization and convergence to suboptimal solutions.

5.8.3 6.8.3 Model Misspecification

VI approximation error can mask model inadequacy. The ELBO provides a lower bound on model evidence, but a high ELBO doesn’t guarantee good model fit.

5.8.4 Example 6.5: Diagnosing VI Pathologies

function diagnose_vi_quality(q_


\newpage

# Chapter 7: Hierarchical and Multilevel Models

Hierarchical models represent one of the most powerful applications of Bayesian inference, allowing us to model complex data structures with multiple levels of variation while sharing information across groups. These models naturally capture the trade-off between complete pooling (treating all groups as identical) and no pooling (treating each group as completely separate) through partial pooling, which shrinks group-specific estimates toward an overall mean.

The key insight underlying hierarchical models is that parameters themselves follow distributions. Rather than treating group effects as fixed but unknown constants, we model them as random variables drawn from a common distribution. This framework provides a principled approach to borrowing strength across groups while respecting their individual characteristics.

## Foundations of Hierarchical Modeling

### The Hierarchical Structure

Consider data organized into J groups, where group j contains n_j observations y_{ij} for i = 1, ..., n_j. A hierarchical model introduces parameters θ_j for each group j, but treats these parameters as draws from a common population distribution.

> **Definition 7.1 (Three-Stage Hierarchical Model).** A three-stage hierarchical model consists of:
> 1. **Data model**: y_{ij} | θ_j ~ f(y_{ij} | θ_j) for i = 1, ..., n_j, j = 1, ..., J
> 2. **Population model**: θ_j | ψ ~ g(θ_j | ψ) for j = 1, ..., J
> 3. **Hyperprior**: ψ ~ h(ψ)

The population model treats the group parameters θ_j as exchangeable, meaning that prior to observing data, we have no reason to distinguish between groups. The hyperparameters ψ control the population distribution and are themselves assigned priors.

### Exchangeability and Partial Pooling

The concept of exchangeability formalizes the idea that groups are similar but not identical.

> **Definition 7.2 (Exchangeability).** Random variables θ_1, ..., θ_J are exchangeable if their joint distribution is invariant under permutations of indices. That is, for any permutation π of {1, ..., J}, the joint distribution of (θ_{π(1)}, ..., θ_{π(J)}) equals that of (θ_1, ..., θ_J).

By de Finetti's theorem, exchangeable sequences have a representation as conditionally independent given some parameter. In hierarchical models, this parameter is ψ.

The posterior for group j parameters combines information from three sources:
1. The within-group likelihood from observations in group j
2. The population model linking θ_j to other groups
3. The hyperprior on ψ

This leads to shrinkage: extreme group estimates are pulled toward the population mean, with the amount of shrinkage depending on the relative precision of within-group and between-group information.

### Example: Normal Hierarchical Model

Consider the canonical normal hierarchical model:

```julia
using Distributions, Turing, StatsPlots, Random

# Simulate hierarchical data
Random.seed!(123)
J = 8  # number of groups
n_j = [10, 12, 8, 15, 9, 11, 13, 10]  # group sizes
μ_true = 5.0  # population mean
τ_true = 2.0  # between-group standard deviation
σ_true = 1.5  # within-group standard deviation

# Generate true group effects
θ_true = rand(Normal(μ_true, τ_true), J)
println("True group effects: ", round.(θ_true, digits=2))

# Generate observations
y = Vector{Vector{Float64}}(undef, J)
for j in 1:J
    y[j] = rand(Normal(θ_true[j], σ_true), n_j[j])
end

# Calculate group sample means for comparison
y_bar = [mean(y[j]) for j in 1:J]
println("Sample means: ", round.(y_bar, digits=2))

Now we define the hierarchical model:

@model function hierarchical_normal(y, n_j, J)
    # Hyperpriors
    μ ~ Normal(0, 10)
    τ ~ Exponential(1)
    σ ~ Exponential(1)
    
    # Group effects
    θ ~ filldist(Normal(μ, τ), J)
    
    # Likelihood
    for j in 1:J
        y[j] ~ filldist(Normal(θ[j], σ), n_j[j])
    end
end

# Fit the model
model = hierarchical_normal(y, n_j, J)
chain = sample(model, NUTS(), 2000)

# Extract posterior summaries
θ_posterior = mean(Array(group(chain, :θ)), dims=1)[1, :]
println("Posterior means: ", round.(θ_posterior, digits=2))
println("True values: ", round.(θ_true, digits=2))

The key phenomenon is shrinkage: posterior means θ_posterior are pulled toward the overall mean compared to the sample means y_bar, with groups having smaller sample sizes experiencing more shrinkage.

5.9 Random Intercepts and Random Slopes

Hierarchical models naturally extend to regression settings where we have covariates. The simplest extension adds group-specific intercepts, but we can also include group-specific slopes.

5.9.1 Random Intercepts Model

For observations y_{ij} with covariates x_{ij} in group j:

y_{ij} | α_j, β, σ² ~ N(α_j + β^T x_{ij}, σ²) α_j | μ_α, τ_α² ~ N(μ_α, τ_α²)

Here β represents population-level (fixed) effects, while α_j are group-specific intercepts.

5.9.2 Random Intercepts and Slopes Model

We can allow both intercepts and slopes to vary by group:

y_{ij} | α_j, β_j, σ² ~ N(α_j + β_j^T x_{ij}, σ²) (α_j, β_j) | μ, Σ ~ N(μ, Σ)

where μ = (μ_α, μ_β) and Σ is the covariance matrix of random effects.

using LinearAlgebra

# Simulate random slopes data
Random.seed!(456)
J = 12
n_j = fill(20, J)
μ_α = 2.0
μ_β = 1.5
σ_α = 0.8
σ_β = 0.4
ρ = 0.3  # correlation between intercepts and slopes
σ_y = 1.0

# Covariance matrix for random effects
Σ = [σ_α^2 ρ*σ_α*σ_β; ρ*σ_α*σ_β σ_β^2]
μ_vec = [μ_α, μ_β]

# Generate random effects
random_effects = [rand(MvNormal(μ_vec, Σ)) for _ in 1:J]
α_true = [re[1] for re in random_effects]
β_true = [re[2] for re in random_effects]

# Generate data
x_all = Float64[]
y_all = Float64[]
group_all = Int[]

for j in 1:J
    x_j = randn(n_j[j])  # standardized covariates
    y_j = α_true[j] .+ β_true[j] .* x_j .+ σ_y * randn(n_j[j])
    
    append!(x_all, x_j)
    append!(y_all, y_j)
    append!(group_all, fill(j, n_j[j]))
end

@model function random_slopes_model(y, x, group, J)
    # Fixed effects
    μ_α ~ Normal(0, 5)
    μ_β ~ Normal(0, 5)
    
    # Random effects variance components
    σ_α ~ Exponential(1)
    σ_β ~ Exponential(1)
    ρ ~ Uniform(-1, 1)
    
    # Residual variance
    σ_y ~ Exponential(1)
    
    # Construct covariance matrix
    σ_αβ = ρ * σ_α * σ_β
    Σ = [[σ_α^2, σ_αβ] [σ_αβ, σ_β^2]]
    
    # Random effects
    random_effects ~ filldist(MvNormal([μ_α, μ_β], Σ), J)
    
    # Extract random intercepts and slopes
    α = [random_effects[j][1] for j in 1:J]
    β = [random_effects[j][2] for j in 1:J]
    
    # Likelihood
    for i in eachindex(y)
        j = group[i]
        y[i] ~ Normal(α[j] + β[j] * x[i], σ_y)
    end
end

# Fit model
model_slopes = random_slopes_model(y_all, x_all, group_all, J)
chain_slopes = sample(model_slopes, NUTS(), 1000)

This model estimates both the population-level means (μ_α, μ_β) and the correlation structure of random effects, allowing for realistic modeling of how intercepts and slopes covary across groups.

5.10 Non-Centered Parameterization

When hierarchical variance components are small or when there are few observations per group, standard parameterizations can lead to poor MCMC mixing due to strong posterior correlations between group effects and hyperparameters.

5.10.1 The Funnel Problem

Consider the standard (centered) parameterization: - θ_j ~ N(μ, τ²) - y_{ij} ~ N(θ_j, σ²)

When τ is small, the posterior geometry becomes funnel-shaped: as τ approaches zero, θ_j becomes tightly concentrated around μ, but the sampler struggles to navigate this narrow region efficiently.

5.10.2 Non-Centered Parameterization

The non-centered parameterization reparameterizes using standardized random effects:

θ_j = μ + τ · η_j η_j ~ N(0, 1)

This transforms the model to: - η_j ~ N(0, 1) (independent of hyperparameters) - θ_j = μ + τ · η_j (deterministic transformation) - y_{ij} ~ N(μ + τ · η_j, σ²)

# Comparison of centered vs non-centered parameterizations

@model function centered_model(y, n_j, J)
    μ ~ Normal(0, 10)
    τ ~ Exponential(1)
    σ ~ Exponential(1)
    
    # Centered parameterization
    θ ~ filldist(Normal(μ, τ), J)
    
    for j in 1:J
        y[j] ~ filldist(Normal(θ[j], σ), n_j[j])
    end
end

@model function noncentered_model(y, n_j, J)
    μ ~ Normal(0, 10)
    τ ~ Exponential(1)
    σ ~ Exponential(1)
    
    # Non-centered parameterization
    η ~ filldist(Normal(0, 1), J)
    θ = μ .+ τ .* η
    
    for j in 1:J
        y[j] ~ filldist(Normal(θ[j], σ), n_j[j])
    end
end

# Create challenging data with small between-group variation
Random.seed!(789)
J_small = 6
n_j_small = fill(5, J_small)
θ_true_small = 5.0 .+ 0.2 * randn(J_small)  # small between-group variation
y_small = [5.0 .+ 0.2 * randn() .+ 1.0 * randn(n_j_small[j]) for j in 1:J_small]

# Compare sampling efficiency
chain_centered = sample(centered_model(y_small, n_j_small, J_small), NUTS(), 1000)
chain_noncentered = sample(noncentered_model(y_small, n_j_small, J_small), NUTS(), 1000)

# Check effective sample sizes (higher is better)
using MCMCChains
ess_centered = ess_rhat(chain_centered)
ess_noncentered = ess_rhat(chain_noncentered)

println("Centered ESS for τ: ", ess_centered[:τ, :ess])
println("Non-centered ESS for τ: ", ess_noncentered[:τ, :ess])

The non-centered parameterization typically shows much better effective sample sizes and R̂ values, especially when τ is small relative to within-group variation.

5.11 Hierarchical Priors for Variance Components

Specifying priors for variance components requires careful consideration, as these parameters are constrained to be positive and their scale affects model behavior significantly.

5.11.1 Common Prior Choices

Definition 7.3 (Weakly Informative Priors for Scale Parameters). For hierarchical models, common choices for variance component priors include: 1. Half-Cauchy: τ ~ Cauchy⁺(0, s) with scale s chosen based on expected variation 2. Exponential: τ ~ Exp(λ) with rate λ chosen to reflect prior beliefs 3. Inverse-Gamma: τ² ~ IG(α, β) (though this can be problematic) 4. Log-Normal: log(τ) ~ N(μ, σ²)

The half-Cauchy prior with appropriate scale has become popular due to its heavy tails (allowing for large between-group variation when warranted) while remaining proper.

5.11.2 Hyperprior Sensitivity

Different hyperpriors can lead to substantially different inferences, especially with limited data:

# Compare different hyperpriors for variance components
@model function varying_hyperprior(y, n_j, J, prior_type)
    μ ~ Normal(0, 10)
    σ ~ Exponential(1)
    
    if prior_type == "halfcauchy"
        τ ~ TruncatedNormal(0, Inf, 0, 2.5)  # Half-Cauchy approximation
    elseif prior_type == "exponential"
        τ ~ Exponential(1)
    elseif prior_type == "lognormal"
        log_τ ~ Normal(0, 1)
        τ = exp(log_τ)
    end
    
    η ~ filldist(Normal(0, 1), J)
    θ = μ .+ τ .* η
    
    for j in 1:J
        y[j] ~ filldist(Normal(θ[j], σ), n_j[j])
    end
    
    return τ
end

# Fit with different priors
priors = ["halfcauchy", "exponential", "lognormal"]
chains_prior = Dict()

for prior in priors
    chains_prior[prior] = sample(varying_hyperprior(y_small, n_j_small, J_small, prior), 
                                NUTS(), 1000)
end

# Compare posterior distributions of τ
for prior in priors
    τ_samples = Array(chains_prior[prior][:τ])
    println("$prior prior - posterior mean τ: ", round(mean(τ_samples), digits=3))
    println("$prior prior - posterior std τ: ", round(std(τ_samples), digits=3))
end

5.12 Meta-Analysis as Hierarchical Modeling

Meta-analysis provides a natural application of hierarchical models, where we combine results from multiple studies while accounting for both within-study and between-study variation.

5.12.1 Random Effects Meta-Analysis

Consider J studies, each providing an effect size estimate ŷ_j with standard error s_j. The random effects meta-analysis model is:

ŷ_j | θ_j, s_j ~ N(θ_j, s_j²) (within-study variation) θ_j | μ, τ² ~ N(μ, τ²) (between-study variation)

where μ represents the overall effect size and τ² the between-study heterogeneity.

# Meta-analysis example: Effect of treatment on blood pressure
Random.seed!(101)

# Simulate meta-analysis data
J_meta = 15
studies = ["Study_$i" for i in 1:J_meta]
sample_sizes = rand(30:200, J_meta)
true_effect = -5.0  # true reduction in blood pressure
between_study_sd = 2.0

# True study effects
true_effects = true_effect .+ between_study_sd * randn(J_meta)

# Observed effect sizes with sampling error
standard_errors = 1.5 ./ sqrt.(sample_sizes)  # SE decreases with sample size
observed_effects = true_effects .+ standard_errors .* randn(J_meta)

@model function meta_analysis(y, se, J)
    # Overall effect
    μ ~ Normal(0, 10)
    
    # Between-study heterogeneity
    τ ~ Exponential(1)
    
    # Study-specific effects
    η ~ filldist(Normal(0, 1), J)
    θ = μ .+ τ .* η
    
    # Likelihood (known standard errors)
    for j in 1:J
        y[j] ~ Normal(θ[j], se[j])
    end
    
    return μ, τ, θ
end

# Fit meta-analysis
meta_model = meta_analysis(observed_effects, standard_errors, J_meta)
meta_chain = sample(meta_model, NUTS(), 2000)

# Extract results
μ_post = Array(meta_chain[:μ])
τ_post = Array(meta_chain[:τ])

println("Overall effect estimate: ", round(mean(μ_post), digits=2), 
        " (95% CI: ", round(quantile(μ_post, 0.025), digits=2), ", ",
        round(quantile(μ_post, 0.975), digits=2), ")")
println("Between-study heterogeneity τ: ", round(mean(τ_post), digits=2))
println("True overall effect: ", true_effect)

# Calculate I² statistic (proportion of variation due to heterogeneity)
typical_se = median(standard_errors)
I_squared = τ_post.^2 ./ (τ_post.^2 .+ typical_se^2)
println("I² (heterogeneity proportion): ", round(mean(I_squared), digits=2))

The model provides both an estimate of the overall treatment effect μ and quantifies the heterogeneity τ between studies. High values of I² (approaching 1) indicate substantial heterogeneity, suggesting that studies may differ systematically.

5.13 Spatial Hierarchical Models

Spatial data often exhibit correlation, where nearby locations tend to have similar outcomes. Hierarchical models can incorporate spatial structure through spatially-structured random effects.

5.13.1 Conditional Autoregressive (CAR) Models

For areal data (regions with defined neighbors), CAR models specify:

θ_i | θ_{-i}, τ² ~ N(ρ ∑{j∈N_i} w{ij} θ_j / ∑{j∈N_i} w{ij}, τ²/n_i)

where N_i denotes neighbors of region i, w_{ij} are spatial weights, and n_i is the number of neighbors.

5.13.2 Gaussian Process Models

For continuous spatial data, Gaussian processes provide a flexible framework:

θ(s) ~ GP(μ(s), C(s, s’))

where C(s, s’) is a covariance function depending on spatial separation.

using Distances

# Simulate spatial data
Random.seed!(202)
n_locations = 25
locations = [(rand()*10, rand()*10) for _ in 1:n_locations]

# True spatial field using Gaussian process
function matern_kernel(d, ℓ, ν=1.5)
    if d == 0
        return 1.0
    else
        scaled_d = sqrt(2*ν) * d / ℓ
        return (2^(1-ν) / gamma(ν)) * scaled_d^ν * besselk(ν, scaled_d)
    end
end

# Covariance matrix
ℓ_true = 2.0  # length scale
σ_spatial = 1.5
distances = [euclidean(locations[i], locations[j]) for i in 1:n_locations, j in 1:n_locations]
K = σ_spatial^2 * [matern_kernel(distances[i,j], ℓ_true) for i in 1:n_locations, j in 1:n_locations]

# Add nugget for numerical stability
K += 1e-6 * I

# Generate spatial random effects
θ_spatial = rand(MvNormal(zeros(n_locations), K))

# Observed data with measurement error
σ_obs = 0.5
y_spatial = θ_spatial + σ_obs * randn(n_locations)

@model function spatial_gp_model(y, locations, n)
    # GP hyperparameters
    μ ~ Normal(0, 5)
    σ_spatial ~ Exponential(1)
    ℓ ~ Exponential(2)
    σ_obs ~ Exponential(1)
    
    # Distance matrix
    D = [euclidean(locations[i], locations[j]) for i in 1:n, j in 1:n]
    
    # Covariance matrix (simplified exponential kernel)
    K = σ_spatial^2 * exp.(-D ./ ℓ) + σ_obs^2 * I + 1e-6 * I
    
    # Spatial field
    θ ~ MvNormal(fill(μ, n), K)
    
    # Observations
    y ~ MvNormal(θ, σ_obs^2 * I)
end

# Note: GP models are computationally intensive; use smaller example
n_small = 10
locations_small = locations[1:n_small]
y_small_spatial = y_spatial[1:n_small]

# Fit spatial model (may be slow)
spatial_model = spatial_gp_model(y_small_spatial, locations_small, n_small)
# spatial_chain = sample(spatial_model, NUTS(), 500)  # Commented for performance

Spatial models require careful consideration of computational complexity, as the covariance matrix inversion scales as O(n³). Approximation methods like inducing points or basis function representations are often necessary for larger datasets.

5.14 Applications: Clinical Trials and Disease Mapping

5.14.1 Multi-Center Clinical Trials

Clinical trials often involve multiple centers, creating natural hierarchical structure. We may expect treatment effects to vary between centers due to different populations, protocols, or measurement procedures.

# Multi-center clinical trial simulation
Random.seed!(303)

# Trial parameters
n_centers = 8
patients_per_arm = [20, 25, 18, 22, 19, 21, 24, 17]  # per center
overall_treatment_effect = 1.2
between_center_sd = 0.4
within_patient_sd = 2.0

# Generate center-specific effects
center_effects_control = randn(n_centers) * 0.3  # baseline heterogeneity
center_effects_treatment = overall_treatment_effect .+ 
                          between_center_sd * randn(n_centers)

# Generate patient data
center_id = Int[]
treatment = Int[]  # 0 = control, 1 = treatment
outcome = Float64[]

for c in 1:n_centers
    n_per_arm = patients_per_arm[c]
    
    # Control arm
    append!(center_id, fill(c, n_per_arm))
    append!(treatment, zeros(Int, n_per_arm))
    append!(outcome, center_effects_control[c] .+ within_patient_sd * randn(n_per_arm))
    
    # Treatment arm
    append!(center_id, fill(c, n_per_arm))
    append!(treatment, ones(Int, n_per_arm))
    append!(outcome, center_effects_treatment[c] .+ within_patient_sd * randn(n_per_arm))
end

@model function multicenter_trial(y, trt, center, n_centers)
    # Fixed effects
    β₀ ~ Normal(0, 5)  # overall control mean
    β₁ ~ Normal(0, 5)  # overall treatment effect
    
    # Random center effects
    σ_center ~ Exponential(1)
    α_center ~ filldist(Normal(0, σ_center), n_centers)  # center-specific intercepts
    
    # Treatment-by-center interaction
    σ_interaction ~ Exponential(1)
    γ_center ~ filldist(Normal(0, σ_interaction), n_centers)
    
    # Residual variance
    σ ~ Exponential(1)
    
    # Linear predictor
    for i in eachindex(y)
        c = center[i]
        μᵢ = β₀ + α_center[c] + (β₁ + γ_center[c]) * trt[i]
        y[i] ~ Normal(μᵢ, σ)
    end
    
    return β₁, σ_interaction
end

# Fit model
trial_model = multicenter_trial(outcome, treatment, center_id, n_centers)
trial_chain = sample(trial_model, NUTS(), 1500)

# Extract treatment effect
treatment_effect_post = Array(trial_chain[:β₁])
interaction_sd_post = Array(trial_chain[:σ_interaction])

println("Overall treatment effect: ", round(mean(treatment_effect_post), digits=2),
        " (95% CI: ", round(quantile(treatment_effect_post, 0.025), digits=2),
        ", ", round(quantile(treatment_effect_post, 0.975), digits=2), ")")
println("Treatment-by-center heterogeneity: ", 
        round(mean(interaction_sd_post), digits=2))
println("True overall effect: ", overall_treatment_effect)

5.14.2 Disease Mapping

Spatial disease mapping models the geographic distribution of disease rates, often using hierarchical models to borrow strength across neighboring regions.

# Simplified disease mapping example
Random.seed!(404)

n_regions = 20
population = rand(1000:10000, n_regions)  # population at risk
baseline_rate = 0.001  # cases per person per year
region_effects = 0.3 * randn(n_regions)  # log-relative risks

# Expected cases
expected_cases = population .* baseline_rate
# Observed cases (Poisson with area-specific rates)
observed_cases = [rand(Poisson(expected_cases[i] * exp(region_effects[i]))) 
                  for i in 1:n_regions]

@model function disease_mapping(cases, expected, n_regions)
    # Global log-rate
    α ~ Normal(log(0.001), 1)
    
    # Spatial random effects
    σ_spatial ~ Exponential(1)
    θ ~ filldist(Normal(0, σ_spatial), n_regions)
    
    # Poisson likelihood
    for i in 1:n_regions
        log_rate = α + θ[i]
        cases[i] ~ Poisson(expected[i] * exp(log_rate))
    end
    
    return θ
end

disease_model = disease_mapping(observed_cases, expected_cases, n_regions)
disease_chain = sample(disease_model, NUTS(), 1000)

# Calculate standardized morbidity ratios (SMR)
θ_post = Array(group(disease_chain, :θ))
SMR_post = exp.(θ_post)  # relative risk
SMR_mean = mean(SMR_post, dims=1)[1, :]

println("Regions with elevated risk (SMR > 1.2):")
for i in 1:n_regions
    if SMR_mean[i] > 1.2
        println("Region $i: SMR = ", round(SMR_mean[i], digits=2))
    end
end

5.15 Exercises

5.15.1 Exercise 7.1 (Computational)

Implement a hierarchical logistic regression model for binary outcomes in Julia. Use the non-centered parameterization and compare its sampling efficiency to the centered version using a dataset with at least 10 groups and varying group sizes.

5.15.2 Exercise 7.2 (Theoretical)

Prove that under a normal hierarchical model with known within-group variance σ², the posterior mean for group j is a weighted average of the group sample mean ȳⱼ and the population mean μ, where the weights depend on the precision of within-group and between-group information.

5.15.3 Exercise 7.3 (Computational)

Simulate and analyze a random slopes model where the correlation between random intercepts and slopes varies across a range of values (-0.8 to 0.8). Investigate how well the model recovers the true correlation and how this affects predictions for new groups.

5.15.4 Exercise 7.4 (Theoretical)

Show that the marginal likelihood in a hierarchical model can be written as an integral over the hyperparameters, and derive the form of this integral for the normal hierarchical model.

5.15.5 Exercise 7.5 (Computational)

Implement a Bayesian meta-analysis comparing three different prior specifications for the between-study heterogeneity parameter τ. Use a real or realistic simulated dataset and discuss how the choice of prior affects conclusions about heterogeneity.

5.15.6 Exercise 7.6 (Computational)

Create a spatial hierarchical model using a conditional autoregressive (CAR) prior. Implement this in Julia and compare it to an independent random effects model using simulated spatial data with known spatial correlation.

5.15.7 Exercise 7.7 (Theoretical)

Derive the full conditional distributions for all parameters in a normal hierarchical model with unknown variance components. Show how these lead to a Gibbs sampling algorithm.

5.15.8 Exercise 7.8 (Computational)

Implement a hierarchical model for count data using a negative binomial distribution with group-specific dispersion parameters. Compare this to a Poisson hierarchical model using overdispersed count data.

5.15.9 Exercise 7.9 (Theoretical)

Prove that exchangeability is preserved under marginalization: if (θ₁, …, θⱼ) are exchangeable, then any subset (θᵢ₁, …, θᵢₖ) is also exchangeable.

5.15.10 Exercise 7.10 (Applied)

Design and implement a complete analysis of a multi-center clinical trial with patient-level covariates, center effects, and treatment-by-center interactions. Include model checking, sensitivity analysis for priors, and clinically meaningful interpretation of results.

5.16 References

Gelman, A., Carlin, J. B., Stern, H. S., Dunson, D. B., Vehtari, A., & Rubin, D. B. (2013). Bayesian Data Analysis (3rd ed.). Chapman and Hall/CRC.

Gelman, A., & Hill, J. (2006). Data Analysis Using Regression and Multilevel/Hierarchical Models. Cambridge University Press.

Banerjee, S., Carlin, B. P., & Gelfand, A. E. (2014). Hierarchical Modeling and Analysis for Spatial Data (2nd ed.). Chapman and Hall/CRC.

Raudenbush, S. W., & Bryk, A. S. (2002). Hierarchical Linear Models: Applications and Data Analysis Methods (2nd ed.). Sage Publications.
